name: The Table
tagline: Mumbai's most beloved fine-dining room — where Colaba's old trust building meets a San Francisco state of mind.
description: Tucked into the ground floor of the Kalapesi Trust Building in Colaba — a stone's throw from the Taj Mahal Palace — The Table has been the benchmark of Mumbai fine dining since 2011. Founded by restaurateurs Jay Yousuf and Gauri Devidayal, it is celebrated for its globally-inspired, farm-to-table philosophy: much of its produce is sourced from its own farm in Alibaug, and the menu changes daily based on what's fresh. The result is a dining room where you might find a vol-au-vent filled with lamb brain and morels alongside a wood-fired sourdough and the creamiest tiramisu in the city. With black-and-white striped flooring, fresh flowers, and a cocktail menu spanning 100+ wine labels, The Table is simultaneously chic and comfortable — the kind of place where a long dinner feels like a privilege.
address: Ground Floor, Kalapesi Trust Building, Apollo Bunder Marg, Colaba, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400039

name: Masque
tagline: A 10-course journey through India's forgotten ingredients — ranked among Asia's 50 best restaurants.
description: Set inside the soaring loft of a former textile mill in Mahalaxmi, Masque is India's most ambitious culinary project. Chef Prateek Sadhu built it on one radical premise: that India's indigenous, foraged, and hyper-seasonal ingredients — black garlic from Ladakh, wild mushrooms from Meghalaya, kokum from the Konkan coast — deserve the same reverence as anything imported from Europe. Every meal is a 10-course tasting menu, and every course is a revelation in restraint and technical precision. The first course is served inside the open kitchen itself, a theatrical touch that sets the tone for what follows. Ranked 23rd on Asia's Best Restaurants list in 2024 and regularly in the global top 100, Masque is not just the best restaurant in Mumbai — it is one of the best in the world.
address: Laxmi Woollen Mills Estate, Dr. E. Moses Road, Mahalaxmi, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400011

name: Indian Accent
tagline: India's most globally recognised restaurant brand — and the Mumbai chapter is worthy of its legend.
description: Indian Accent Mumbai sits inside the National Museum of Art, Culture and Commerce (NMACC) in BKC, and the setting alone signals ambition. The brainchild of chef Manish Mehrotra — whose New Delhi flagship has been on the World's 50 Best extended list for years — Indian Accent is the finest expression of modern Indian fine dining available anywhere. The chef-led tasting menus take beloved Indian comfort food and strip it down to its essence, then rebuild it with a precision that is quietly breathtaking. Dahi papdi chaat reimagined as a crispy tart; tandoori bacon with refried lentils; meetha achaar pork ribs. The ingredient quality is impeccable, the service is flawless, and the wine pairing programme is one of the strongest in the country.
address: NMACC, G5 Block, Bandra Kurla Complex, Bandra East, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400051

name: The Bombay Canteen
tagline: The late Chef Floyd Cardoz's most enduring legacy — modern India on a plate, with a Mumbai soul.
description: When The Bombay Canteen opened in Kamala Mills in 2015, it changed what Mumbaikars expected from a restaurant. The late Floyd Cardoz and his team — including chefs Thomas Zacharias and Hussain Shahzad — created a menu that wasn't 'fusion' in the cringe-worthy sense, but something more honest: a deep love for regional Indian cooking, expressed with restaurant-level craft and none of the stuffy pretension. The space itself channels a 1960s Bombay bungalow — high ceilings, stained glass windows, long communal tables — and the energy is always buzzing. Ranked 71st in Asia's top 100 restaurants in 2024, it remains one of the most joyful places to eat in the city. The cocktail programme, built around Indian ingredients and home spirits, is one of the best in Mumbai.
address: Unit 1, Process House, S.B. Road, Kamala Mills Compound, Lower Parel, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400013

name: Ekaa
tagline: A singular ingredient — elevated to something extraordinary. Fort's most exciting table.
description: Ekaa, meaning 'one' in Sanskrit, is built on a single philosophy: that each course celebrates a hero ingredient — one vegetable, one protein, one spice — stripped of distraction and coaxed into its fullest expression. Housed in a quietly elegant space in Fort, Chef Niyati Rao's restaurant is one of the most creatively daring in the city. The tasting menus change seasonally and have included concepts as theatrical as 'Kingdoms' — where each course is inspired by a historical dynasty — and as intimate as a single variety of mushroom expressed in five different textures. Ekaa is also one of the most vegan-friendly fine dining restaurants in Mumbai, with thoughtful plant-based menus that never feel like a compromise. The cocktail bar attached to the restaurant is equally excellent.
address: 13, Minoo Desai Marg, Behind Regal Cinema, Colaba, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400001

name: O Pedro
tagline: Made in Goa for the city of Mumbai — the surest escape from Maximum City without leaving BKC.
description: O Pedro, nestled behind frangipani trees in the Jet Airways-Godrej BKC complex, is the restaurant Mumbaikars dream of on Monday mornings. It is the second offering from the Hunger Inc. team behind The Bombay Canteen, and it carries the same love for Indian culinary heritage — but this time focused entirely on Goa and its Portuguese past. The space is airy and tropical, the cocktails are sharp and inventive (the coconut feni Negroni is a religion unto itself), and the food reads like a love letter to the sunshine state. Soft shell crab kismoor, banana leaf-roasted prawns, chicken cafreal, bebinca for dessert — these are not approximations of Goan food, they are Goan food, recalibrated for a restaurant setting without losing any soul.
address: Unit 2, Plot C-68, Jet Airways Godrej BKC Building, Bandra Kurla Complex, Bandra East, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400051

name: Bandra Born
tagline: Chef Gresham Fernandes' love letter to Bandra — graffiti walls gave way to starbursts, but the soul stayed.
description: Bandra Born started as a 12-week pop-up and the city refused to let it close. Chef Gresham Fernandes, who grew up in Bandra, built a restaurant around the flavours, communities, and street-side memories that shaped the suburb — and then won national awards for it. Set in a 3-storeyed building on Chapel Road, the restaurant has a personality you feel the moment you walk in: '90s hip-hop on the sound system, patterned glass windows that catch passing headlights like shooting stars, a mahua flower Negroni in hand. The food is generous, bold, and deeply personal — bone marrow chilli cheese toast, duck stroopwafel, slow-cooked king cabbage served in its own brown-butter jus. The prawn butter garlic toast alone is worth the trip.
address: Rose Minar, 87 Chapel Road, Annexe, Reclamation, Bandra West, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400050

name: Trishna
tagline: The butter garlic crab that Bollywood stars and business elites squeeze into a back alley to eat.
description: In the narrow lanes behind Kala Ghoda, Trishna has been the definitive pilgrimage for seafood lovers since 1981. There are no reservations on weeknights, no pretension, and no guarantee of a quiet table — but there is crab. Fresh jumbo crab cooked with lashings of butter, black pepper, and garlic in quantities that border on the irresponsible. The World's 50 Best Discovery programme lists it as one of Mumbai's essential experiences, and every serious food traveller who has been to the city has a Trishna story. The menu is overwhelming at first — there are reportedly 21 sauce options for king prawns alone — but that abundance is part of the charm. The seafood biryani, the Bombay duck (bombil) fry, and the Hyderabadi pomfret are all legendary. Go hungry. Go loud.
address: 7, Sai Baba Marg, Kala Ghoda, Fort, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400023

name: Mahesh Lunch Home
tagline: The original Mangalorean seafood institution — where the surmai curry has been breaking hearts since 1977.
description: S.C. Karkera opened the first Mahesh Lunch Home in Fort in 1977 to serve Mangalorean food to office workers and expats missing the taste of home. What he built, over the following decades, became one of the most beloved seafood restaurants in India. The flagship Fort location remains the pilgrimage point: a no-frills, two-level dining room where the fish is always fresh (guests can view it before it is cooked), the butter garlic crab is legendary, and a meal with drinks rarely crosses the price of a quiet afternoon well spent. The surmai curry with neer dosa, the ghee roast, and the kori rotti are touchstones of Mangalorean cooking, prepared here with consistency and care. Celebrities and office-goers share the same tables. That's the Mahesh way.
address: 8-B, Cawasji Patel Street, Fort, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400001

name: Britannia & Co.
tagline: Mumbai's most beloved Parsi table — open since 1923, unchanged by design, irreplaceable by nature.
description: In 1923, Rashid Kohinoor opened Britannia & Co. in the lanes of Ballard Estate, and almost nothing has changed since. The chequered tablecloths are original. The Bentwood chairs are original. The portraits of the British Royal Family on the walls are original — and so, apparently, is the raspberry soda. Run today by nonagenarian patriarch Boman Kohinoor, who still makes daily appearances, Britannia is the most irreplaceable restaurant in Mumbai. Its Berry Pulao — fragrant basmati rice cooked with tart Iranian barberries imported specifically for this dish — is a bucket list item for every serious food lover in the country. The dhansak, the sali boti, and the caramel custard are equally non-negotiable. Arrive by noon. There are no reservations, and the food runs out.
address: Wakefield House, Sprott Road, Opposite New Customs House, Ballard Estate, Fort, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400001

name: Leopold Cafe
tagline: Founded in 1871, immortalised in Shantaram — the most storied cafe in Mumbai.
description: Leopold Cafe on Colaba Causeway is one of the oldest surviving restaurants in India, founded in 1871 by Iranian Zoroastrian immigrants who initially used the space as a cooking oil store and pharmacy before it became the legendary bar-and-restaurant it is today. The cafe is famous the world over partly for its appearance in Gregory David Roberts' novel Shantaram — but also for its extraordinary defiance on the night of the 2008 Mumbai attacks, when it reopened just four days after terrorists opened fire inside its walls. Today, Leopold is a cultural landmark as much as a restaurant: a warm-lit, high-ceilinged room where backpackers and Bollywood stars and old-timers share the same long tables, the beer flows freely, and the menu covers everything from butter chicken to fish and chips.
address: Colaba Causeway, Colaba, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400005

name: Cafe Mondegar
tagline: Mario Miranda's murals, Mumbai's first jukebox, and a cold Kingfisher — since 1932.
description: Mondy's, as every Mumbaikar calls it, was born in 1932 in the reception of the old Apollo Hotel on Colaba Causeway — a modest Irani café that, over nine decades, became one of the city's most cherished institutions. The transformation came in the 1990s when owner Rusi Yazdegardi commissioned legendary Goan cartoonist Mario Miranda to paint the walls. Miranda's murals — vivid, humorous, densely populated scenes of everyday Mumbai life — now cover every surface, making the cafe as much an art gallery as a restaurant. Add the city's first jukebox (still playing in the corner), communal wooden tables, and cold draught beer, and you have a room that is impossible to replicate. The food is cheerful bar food — sizzlers, butter garlic prawns, grilled sandwiches — but nobody comes to Mondegar for the food. They come for the feeling.
address: 5A, Metro House, Shaheed Bhagat Singh Road, Colaba, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400039

name: Kyani & Co.
tagline: Mumbai's oldest Irani café — open since 1904, where time has the good sense to stop.
description: Kyani & Co. has been feeding Mumbai since 1904, making it the city's oldest surviving Irani café. Founded by Mr. Khodram opposite Metro Cinema in Marine Lines, it began as a pure bakery — bread, biscuits, and mawa cakes — and only expanded to meals in 1995. Today, the menu is a small and perfect thing: bun maska, Irani chai, mutton kheema pav, akuri (Parsi scrambled eggs), chicken cutlets, and desserts — caramel custard, cherry custard, mawa cake — that have not changed in decades. The interior is all mirrored walls, high ceilings, checkered table cloths, and wooden chairs that probably hold decades of stories. The servers, some of whom have worked here for forty years, might know your order before you say it. Cash only. No WiFi. No pretension.
address: Jer Mahal Building, 657, Jagannath Shankar Seth Road, Opposite Metro Cinema, Marine Lines, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400002

name: Aaswad
tagline: London's Foodie Hub called its Misal the world's best vegetarian food. Dadar has known this for decades.
description: Aaswad in Dadar is not a restaurant you visit for the ambience. The seating is communal, the decor is bare, and the service is rapid and no-nonsense. But every single thing on the menu is, without qualification, extraordinary — and Mumbai knows it. The Misal Pav here has been awarded the best vegetarian food in the world by London's Foodie Hub Global Awards, and the recognition is deserved: a thick, fiery usal of sprouted moth beans swimming in a tarri (spiced oil gravy), topped with chivda and farsan, served with soft pav and a squeeze of lime. The batata vada, pithla bhakri, sabudana vada, modak, and sol kadhi are equally brilliant. Aaswad is the most authentic, essential, and irreplaceable expression of Maharashtrian home cooking available to the public in Mumbai.
address: Sadashiv Cross Lane, Near Shivaji Mandir, Dadar West, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400028

name: Swati Snacks
tagline: The snack bar that made Mumbai fall in love with Gujarati street food — one patra ni machhi at a time.
description: Swati Snacks on Tardeo Road is a Mumbai institution so beloved that people plan their day around it. The modest, brightly lit restaurant has been serving immaculate Gujarati snack food since 1963, and the queue outside at any given mealtime tells you everything about its standing. Unlike Gujarati restaurants that pursue comprehensiveness, Swati is focused and precise — a tight menu of chaats, sevpuri, handvo, patra, and the extraordinary dahi puri that has its own fan following. The kitchen works with obsessive quality control: ingredients are fresh, spicing is calibrated, and every dish arrives tasting exactly as it should. The panki (rice batter steamed inside banana leaf), the dal dhokli, and the undhiyu are seasonal specialities that regulars plan around.
address: 248, Karai Estate, Near Bhatia Hospital, Tardeo, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400007

name: Tanjore Tiffin Room
tagline: A Tamil dinner inside someone's home — except better, and in Versova.
description: Tanjore Tiffin Room recreates an experience so intimate it is almost disorienting in a restaurant context: the feeling of being fed by a Tamil family who genuinely love you. The restaurant serves authentic Tamil Nadu cuisine — not the dosa-idli South Indian shorthand familiar everywhere else, but the real regional cooking of the Kaveri delta: kuzhambu, kootu, rasam, rice preparations, and chutneys that people who grew up in Tamil homes weep over with recognition. The food at TTR is deeply researched, cooked from family recipes, and served with a warmth that no amount of training can replicate. It has a loyal, almost cult-like following, and tables book out weeks in advance on weekends. The thali here is one of the finest in Mumbai.
address: Shop 3-4, Yari Road, Near Versova Market, Versova, Andheri West, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400061

name: Masala Library
tagline: Indian food deconstructed and rebuilt — where science meets spice at BKC.
description: Chef Jiggs Kalra and the Massive Restaurants group built Masala Library around a single audacious question: what happens when you apply the techniques of molecular gastronomy to Indian cooking? The answer, at its best, is extraordinary — spherified pani puri that bursts in your mouth like a flavour bomb, tandoori chicken powder served on edible soil, dal makhani slow-cooked for 48 hours. The tasting menu here is one of the most theatrical dining experiences in the city, and the à la carte is equally inventive. Set in an elegant, library-themed space in BKC, Masala Library attracts corporate diners, food obsessives, and anyone marking an occasion that demands the spectacular.
address: Ground Floor, First International Financial Centre, G Block BKC, Bandra Kurla Complex, Bandra East, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400051

name: Jamavar
tagline: India's royal kitchen, translated into a dining room at The Leela — where the rogan josh has no rival.
description: Jamavar at The Leela Mumbai is the hotel's signature Indian restaurant, and one of the finest places in the city to eat the cooking of North India and Kashmir. The room is gorgeous — dark wood, silk furnishings, soft lighting — and the menu reads like a tour through the royal kitchens of the Mughal court. The rogan josh is slow-cooked until the lamb surrenders entirely to its aromatic gravy. The dal bukhara (black lentils slow-cooked for 18 hours over a wood fire) is the most consistently excellent version in Mumbai. The breads — roomali roti, sheermal, laccha paratha — are made fresh to order in a dedicated bread kitchen. For an occasion dinner that demands both grandeur and substance, Jamavar delivers.
address: The Leela Mumbai, Sahar Airport Road, Andheri East, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400059

name: Sea Lounge
tagline: High tea with the Gateway of India framed in the window — the most civilised two hours in Mumbai.
description: The Sea Lounge at the Taj Mahal Palace Hotel is not a restaurant in the ordinary sense. It is an experience that has remained largely unchanged since the hotel opened in 1903 — a high-ceilinged, plush-seated lounge on the first floor of the heritage wing, with floor-to-ceiling windows that frame the Gateway of India and the Arabian Sea beyond in a composition so perfect it looks staged. The afternoon high tea here — sandwiches, scones, cakes, and savoury bites, all accompanied by a trolley of estate teas — is one of Mumbai's finest rituals. The cold coffee is legendary, the pani puri is arguably the most incongruous item on a five-star menu and inexplicably perfect, and the service is the kind of attentive that makes you feel, for a few hours, that the world outside does not exist.
address: Taj Mahal Palace Hotel, Apollo Bunder, Colaba, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400001

name: Peshwari
tagline: The most serious meat restaurant in Mumbai — and the dal that has made grown men emotional.
description: Peshwari at the ITC Maratha is the Mumbai outpost of the legendary ITC Hotels signature Indian restaurant brand, and it is the city's most uncompromising destination for North-West Frontier cooking. The menu here is almost willfully brief — a short list of kebabs, breads, and curries, all cooked over a live charcoal tandoor and wood fire. The Gosht Dum Biryani is one of the best in the city. The Dal Bukhara — ITC's signature, cooked for 18 hours on a slow fire — has been declared one of the great dishes of Indian cooking by food writers across the world. Seek out the murgh Malai tikka, the seekh kebab, and the roomali roti, and arrive with the understanding that this is not fusion or innovation — it is craft at a very high level.
address: ITC Maratha, Sahar Airport Road, Andheri East, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400099

name: Bayroute
tagline: The most beautiful Middle Eastern dining room in India — and the hummus to match.
description: Bayroute in Cuffe Parade is Mumbai's finest Middle Eastern restaurant, and one of the most visually spectacular dining rooms in the city. The space is designed around the imagery of a souk at dusk — arched colonnades, warm amber lighting, handcrafted lanterns, and an open kitchen where wood-fired flatbreads emerge in steady succession. The food is proudly Lebanese: mezze spreads of labneh, baba ghanoush, and muhamarra that would pass muster in Beirut, followed by shawarma preparations, slow-roasted lamb ouzi, and grilled meats that arrive at the table still fragrant with wood smoke. The cocktail programme draws from North African and Levantine botanicals. For a special-occasion dinner that demands beauty, Bayroute consistently delivers.
address: Empire Plaza, LBS Marg, Chandivali, Opposite Blue Diamond Hotel, Cuffe Parade, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400005

name: Americano
tagline: Kala Ghoda's most satisfying pizzeria — where dough ferments for four days and every ricotta is made in-house.
description: Americano in Kala Ghoda is the kind of Italian restaurant that earns repeat visits through sheer quality of craft rather than spectacle. The pizza dough is fermented for four days, developing a depth of flavour and a char on the crust that you taste the difference in immediately. The pastas are made fresh daily in the kitchen. The ricotta is made in-house. These are not details that get shouted about — they are choices that show up plate after plate, visit after visit. Americano has appeared on Asia's Best Restaurants list for both food and drinks, and the cocktail programme is one of the smartest in South Mumbai. The Pink Panther pizza, the Pici Pomodoro, and the Black Truffle Cappelletti are non-negotiables.
address: Ador House, K. Dubash Marg, Kala Ghoda, Fort, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400023

name: Gajalee
tagline: The definitive Malvani seafood restaurant — where the Konkan coast comes to Vile Parle.
description: Gajalee has been serving Malvani coastal food in Mumbai since 1976, and it is regarded by many food critics as the finest expression of this cuisine available in the city. Malvani cooking — from the Konkan coast of Maharashtra and Goa — is characterised by the use of fresh coconut, Malvani masala, and coastal produce: pomfret, surmai, prawns, crabs, and the beloved bombil (Bombay duck). Gajalee prepares all of it with exceptional skill. The Sol Kadi is one of the best in the city — cool, tangy, and deeply refreshing. The Malvani crab curry, the prawn koliwada, and the pomfret recheado are the benchmarks by which every other Malvani restaurant in Mumbai measures itself. The dining room is large, warmly lit, and perpetually full — always a good sign.
address: Hotel Jewel of India, 1-A, Hanuman Road, Vile Parle East, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400057

name: Cafe Madras
tagline: Matunga's oldest South Indian breakfast institution — the filter coffee alone makes the trip.
description: Matunga is Mumbai's South Indian heartland, and Cafe Madras has been its breakfast anchor since 1940. The restaurant occupies a corner position in the bustling market area and has the particular energy of a place that has fed the same families for three generations: fast service, no-frills seating, and a menu of South Indian classics executed at a level that more expensive restaurants rarely match. The idli here is fermented longer and steamed more precisely than almost anywhere else in the city — cloud-soft, with a faint sour note. The dosa is properly lacy and crisp. The filter coffee arrives in a stainless dabara-tumbler set, the way it should. Matunga's South Indian community has kept this place honest and exceptional for over eight decades.
address: 38-C, Circle House, Lakhamsi Napoo Road, Matunga East, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400019

name: Jimmy Boy
tagline: Fort's most generous Parsi table — where the dhansak is the real reason offices empty at noon.
description: Jimmy Boy in Fort is the city's most accessible and consistent Parsi restaurant, and for the thousands of office workers in South Mumbai who eat here on weekday lunches, it is an anchor of the week. The restaurant is named after the Parsi tradition of calling a beloved young man 'Jimmy Boy', and the food carries that familial warmth. The dhansak — the definitive Parsi dish of dal slow-cooked with mutton, vegetables, and spices until they become a single, complex whole, served with caramelised brown rice — is one of the best in the city. The salli boti, the chicken farcha, and the patra ni machhi (fish steamed in banana leaves with green chutney) are equally authoritative. For visitors who want to understand Parsi food without booking an institution like Britannia, Jimmy Boy is the answer.
address: 11, Bank Street, Horniman Circle, Fort, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400023

name: CinCin
tagline: BKC's most celebrated Italian bar — where after-work hours are worth the traffic.
description: CinCin (pronounced 'chin-chin', the Italian toast) is the kind of Italian bar that BKC needed: relaxed, stylish, and genuinely good. Set in a double-height space with a long marble bar and a wood-fired pizza oven visible from every table, it manages to be simultaneously a proper restaurant and a proper cocktail bar. The Aperol spritz is poured correctly (Aperol, Prosecco, soda, a splash of blood orange juice). The Neapolitan-style pizzas are excellent. The fresh pastas and the charcuterie board are reasons to extend the evening. CinCin is most magical on a Tuesday evening when the corporate crush has thinned and the marble bar stools are occupied by people having genuinely good conversations.
address: Ground Floor, Naman Midtown, Senapati Bapat Marg, Elphinstone Road West, Lower Parel, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400013

name: The Bombay Cartel
tagline: Lower Parel's most spirited vegetarian restaurant — rooftop, live music, and a cheese fondue that earns its place.
description: The Bombay Cartel in Lower Parel has built an enviable reputation as a destination restaurant for vegetarians who refuse to compromise on ambience or creativity. The multi-floor space includes a ground floor restaurant, a cosy bar section, and a rooftop with city views. The menu spans continents — avocado hummus toast, paneer tikka pizza, sushi, sizzlers, pasta — executed with care and a willingness to experiment that other vegetarian restaurants in the city rarely match. The drinks programme, with customised cocktails and mocktails, is thoughtful. Live music on weekends and a regularly changing specials menu keep regulars returning. The Cannelloni Skillet Cookies for dessert are a signature that has achieved a small cult following.
address: Todi Mills Compound, Mathuradas Mill Road, Lower Parel, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400013

name: Taftoon Bar & Kitchen
tagline: Named for a Persian flatbread, Taftoon is BKC's most atmospheric bar with actual culinary ambition.
description: Taftoon in BKC takes its name from the Persian word for the flatbread baked in clay ovens, and the restaurant's aesthetic extends that reference — arched brick, Persian tiles, low lighting, and a long bar stocked with serious spirits. But Taftoon is not content to coast on atmosphere. The food is genuinely good: Persian-inflected kebabs, moreish mezze, wood-fired breads, and a short selection of modern Indian dishes that sit comfortably alongside the Middle Eastern framework. The cocktail programme is one of the stronger ones in the BKC corridor. For Friday evening drinks that transition naturally into a proper dinner, Taftoon has few rivals.
address: Trade Centre, Bandra Kurla Complex, Bandra East, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400051

name: Candies
tagline: Bandra's most beloved neighbourhood café — where the carrot cake is a non-negotiable part of Sunday.
description: Candies started in 2001 as a small bakery on Pali Hill in Bandra, opened by Allan and Rhea Bhatiwala. Over the following two decades it expanded and evolved but never lost the thing that made it special: it feels like a home. The mismatched furniture, the art on the walls, the small garden, the counter of cakes and baked goods you pass on the way in — all of it communicates a warmth that corporate restaurants spend millions trying to manufacture. The food covers a lot of ground (sandwiches, pasta, grills, breakfast all day) and most of it is very good. But the cakes — carrot cake, blueberry cheesecake, red velvet — are extraordinary. Candies is where Bandra has birthday parties, first dates, quiet Sundays, and difficult conversations.
address: Pali Naka, St. John the Baptist Road, Bandra West, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400050

name: Peshwa Pavilion
tagline: The only luxury buffet in Mumbai where the Maharashtrian section is the reason you came.
description: Peshwa Pavilion at the ITC Maratha is Mumbai's most distinguished buffet restaurant, but calling it a buffet sells it short. The spread here is an anthology of Indian regional cooking, executed at a quality level that rivals à la carte restaurants. The Maharashtrian section — sol kadhi, bharli vangi, pithla, kombdi vade, and the extraordinary Malvani fish curry — is the finest in any hotel setting in the city. The North Indian and Mughlai sections are equally serious. The live counter for dosas and parathas is permanently attended by a chef who will make yours fresh. Breakfast at Peshwa Pavilion, with its extraordinary bread basket and fresh fruit counter, is one of the great quiet pleasures of a stay at the ITC Maratha.
address: ITC Maratha, Sahar Airport Road, Andheri East, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400099

name: Prithvi Café
tagline: The theatre café where Juhu's artists, directors, and dreamers have gathered since 1978.
description: Prithvi Café is attached to the Prithvi Theatre, the cultural landmark built by Shashi Kapoor in Juhu, and it is inseparable from it. You come here before or after a play, or sometimes instead of one, to sit in the open courtyard under the trees and eat simple food with a cup of chai while the city's creative community drifts in and out. The food — sandwiches, pasta, Maggi, chai and coffee — is honest and unpretentious. The magic is not on the plate but in the atmosphere: the particular energy of a place that has hosted actors, poets, filmmakers, and students for over four decades, all in the shade of the same old trees. It is one of the most peaceful outdoor seating experiences available anywhere in Mumbai.
address: Prithvi Theatre, Janki Kutir, Juhu Church Road, Juhu, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400049

name: KOKO
tagline: Lower Parel's most glamorous Asian restaurant — where dim sum and disco lights coexist magnificently.
description: KOKO in Kamala Mills is the most stylish Pan-Asian restaurant in Mumbai — a cavernous, multi-level space that does not choose between being a serious restaurant and a serious bar, and succeeds at both. The food programme is split between a Japanese counter, a dim sum operation, and a larger Chinese-inspired menu, all executed at a level that would be respectable in Tokyo or Hong Kong. The crystal dumplings with black truffle, the truffle edamame, and the wagyu gyoza are the hits that keep the regulars coming back. The cocktail programme, designed around Asian spirits and botanicals, is one of the most creative in the city. KOKO is at its best on a Friday night when the room is full, the sound system is excellent, and the servers seem to enjoy working there — which says something.
address: Kamala Mills Compound, Lower Parel, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400013

name: Bastian
tagline: Bandra's most beloved seafood and steak restaurant — the lobster mac and cheese alone justifies the queue.
description: Bastian opened in Bandra in 2015 and within a year had a queue that stretched down the lane. The restaurant built its reputation on a simple promise: the best quality seafood and meat in the city, cooked with confidence and served without pretension. The lobster mac and cheese is the dish that every first-time visitor orders and every regular keeps returning for. The seafood platter — a spread of oysters, prawns, lobster, and crab that arrives on a bed of ice — is the benchmark for special-occasion dining in Bandra. The burgers are among the best in the city, the cocktail list is intelligent, and the desserts — particularly the Basque cheesecake — are worth saving room for. Reservations are essential on weekends and strongly advised on weekdays.
address: Unit 2, New Kamal Building, Linking Road, Bandra West, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400050

name: Dakshinayan
tagline: The most authentic multi-regional South Indian thali in Mumbai — Juhu's quiet culinary gem.
description: Dakshinayan in Juhu is the rare South Indian restaurant in Mumbai that attempts — and succeeds at — presenting the full range of South India's regional cuisines rather than defaulting to the North Indian idea of 'South Indian food'. The thali here rotates through Kerala, Tamil Nadu, Andhra, and Karnataka cooking traditions, and the quality is consistently high. The Malabar fish curry, the Chettinad chicken, the Andhra prawn masala, and the array of chutneys and pickles that arrive with each thali are prepared with evident care and regional authenticity. For Mumbaikars who grew up with South Indian home cooking, Dakshinayan is one of the few restaurants in the city that tastes like memory.
address: Plot 60-C, Nehru Road, Near Juhu Post Office, Juhu, Santacruz West, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400049

name: Fifty-Five East
tagline: Grand Hyatt's all-day-dining institution — where the live counter theatre is worth the price of admission.
description: Fifty-Five East at the Grand Hyatt Mumbai is the hotel's flagship all-day dining restaurant, and it sits at the apex of Mumbai's luxury buffet scene. The name refers to the GPS coordinates of the hotel's Santacruz location, and the philosophy — bringing together 55 flavours from across the world — is executed with genuine investment. The live cooking stations are the heart of the experience: a tandoor, a pasta counter, a sushi bar, and a wok station all operate simultaneously, and the quality at each is significantly above what most five-star buffets provide. The dessert spread, which includes petit fours, fresh-made gelato, and a rotating selection of Indian sweets, is one of the most satisfying in the city.
address: Grand Hyatt Mumbai, Off Western Express Highway, Santacruz East, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400055

name: Saffron
tagline: JW Marriott Juhu's crown jewel — a signature Indian restaurant with an open tandoor and no compromises.
description: Saffron at the JW Marriott Juhu is Mumbai's most quietly distinguished hotel Indian restaurant — a place where the food is serious enough to draw diners who are not staying at the hotel, and the setting is grand enough to make any occasion feel significant. The open kitchen, where you can watch the tandoor chefs at work, is the theatrical heart of the restaurant. The butter chicken here is a benchmark — not a shortcut curry but a slow-cooked, smoky, deeply layered dish that reminds you why the original is still the greatest. The biryani, the dal makhani, and the tasting menu of regional Indian specialties are all exceptional. The service is immaculate and unhurried.
address: JW Marriott Mumbai Juhu, Juhu Tara Road, Juhu, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400049

name: Oh! Calcutta
tagline: Kolkata's kitchen — transported to Mumbai with the mustard oil still hot.
description: Oh! Calcutta is the finest Bengali restaurant in Mumbai, a city that has a large and fiercely opinionated Bengali population who will settle for nothing less. The restaurant has been serving authentic Bengali food for over two decades, and the consistency it has maintained across that time is remarkable. The Ilish Bhapa (hilsa steamed in mustard and green chilli inside banana leaf) is the dish every Bengali orders first, and it is prepared here with the precision and respect that the hilsa — 'queen of fish', irreplaceable and deeply seasonal — demands. The chingri malaikari, the kosha mangsho, the mishti doi, and the sandesh are all benchmarks. For non-Bengalis encountering this cuisine for the first time, Oh! Calcutta is a revelation.
address: Todi Mills Compound, Lower Parel, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400013

name: Flurys
tagline: Kolkata's 98-year-old tea room — and its Colaba outpost is as faithful to the original as distance allows.
description: Flurys was founded in Kolkata in 1927 by J. Flury, a Swiss confectioner, and for nearly a century it has been the most beloved patisserie and tea room in that city. The Mumbai branch in Colaba maintains the DNA: Art Deco interiors, a long counter of cakes and pastries, all-day breakfast service, and a tea and coffee menu that takes itself seriously. The patisserie is outstanding — the cream puffs, the rum cake, the cheese straws, and the chicken vol-au-vent are made to the original Kolkata recipes. For anyone who grew up with Flurys in Kolkata and now lives in Mumbai, this is a small act of restoration. For everyone else, it is an exceptionally good cafe in one of the most pleasant parts of South Mumbai.
address: Arthur Bunder Road, Colaba, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400005

name: Kailash Parbat
tagline: The oldest chaat restaurant in Mumbai — Sindhi cooking at its most honest and delicious.
description: Kailash Parbat on Colaba Causeway opened in 1952 and has been serving Sindhi vegetarian food and chaat to an ever-loyal clientele ever since. The pani puri here is legendary — the water is spiced differently from the standard Mumbai variety, with a more complex, herb-forward flavour that reflects the Sindhi culinary tradition. The sev puri, dahi bhalla, and papdi chaat are equally excellent. Beyond the chaat counter, the Sindhi dal pakwan (crisp fried puris served with sweet-sour dal) and the aloo tuk (twice-fried potatoes with tamarind chutney) are regional specialities that most Mumbaikars have never encountered anywhere else. The restaurant has several branches across the city and has also expanded internationally, but the original Colaba location remains the favourite.
address: Sheela Mahal, Colaba Causeway, Colaba, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400005

name: Rue Du Liban
tagline: The most intimate Lebanese restaurant in the city — where the mezze is a meditation.
description: Rue Du Liban is a small, carefully curated Lebanese restaurant in Mumbai that has earned a devoted following through the sheer quality and authenticity of its cooking. Unlike the larger, more theatrical Lebanese restaurants in the city, Rue Du Liban is intimate — perhaps 30 covers — and the focus is entirely on the food. The mezze spreads are exceptional: the muhammara (walnut and roasted red pepper dip), the labneh with za'atar, and the perfectly charred pita bread form a combination that makes everything else feel like an afterthought. The main courses — wood-fired lamb, seafood preparations, and vegetable dishes rooted in the Levantine kitchen — are equally precise. The short, thoughtful cocktail list complements the food without overwhelming it.
address: Hill Road, Bandra West, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400050

name: Copper Chimney
tagline: The original North Indian restaurant of Mumbai — open since 1972 and still the tandoor standard.
description: Copper Chimney was founded in 1972 in Worli and is one of Mumbai's oldest continuously operating North Indian restaurants. Over five decades it has watched the restaurant landscape transform around it while remaining committed to the thing it does best: tandoor cooking of the highest order. The murgh tikka, the seekh kebabs, the naan, and the butter chicken have a consistency and quality that never disappoints — not because the restaurant is resting on its laurels, but because it has had fifty years to perfect the craft. The contemporary interiors (updated over the years while retaining warmth) and the efficient, knowledgeable service make it the kind of place that works equally well for a business lunch, a family dinner, or a solo meal at the bar.
address: Dr. Annie Besant Road, Worli, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400018

name: Soam
tagline: The most elegant Gujarati-Rajasthani restaurant in South Mumbai — where the dal baati churma is a ceremony.
description: Soam in Girgaum is a rare thing: a restaurant that treats Gujarati and Rajasthani vegetarian cooking with the same rigour and presentation that fine dining restaurants apply to European food. The dal baati churma here — wheat balls baked in a wood fire, served with five-pulse dal and the sweetened churma crumble — is one of the finest versions available anywhere in Mumbai. The Gujarati thali (rotating daily, always seasonal) is a complete meal in itself: seven or eight small katoris of dal, sabzi, kadhi, rice, pickles, and rotis that arrive in a quiet but relentless succession until you call a halt. The crisp kachori and the patra (taro leaf rolls with tamarind-jaggery filling) are favourite starters. No alcohol is served, which focuses the room entirely on the food.
address: Sadguru Sadan, G. Deshmukh Marg, Near August Kranti Maidan, Girgaum, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400007

name: Trèsind Mumbai
tagline: The Dubai restaurant that took on India — and quietly became one of the finest tasting menu restaurants in the city.
description: Trèsind is a Dubai-based restaurant group that brought its award-winning progressive Indian tasting menu concept to Mumbai, and the transition has been seamless. The concept here is theatrical Indian fine dining: tasting menus that use the full vocabulary of contemporary cooking — fermentation, dehydration, smoke, and temperature play — to express Indian flavours in ways that feel simultaneously surprising and deeply familiar. The service is among the best in Mumbai, with staff who can articulate the story behind every ingredient on every plate. For diners who have done Masque and Ekaa and are looking for the next frontier, Trèsind is the answer. It has consistently earned comparisons to the best progressive Indian restaurants in the world.
address: Lower Parel, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400013

name: Sarvi
tagline: The Bhendi Bazaar institution where the nalli nihari has been calling the city awake since 1945.
description: Sarvi in Bhendi Bazaar is the kind of restaurant that proves a point about Mumbai's culinary identity: that some of the city's finest food is found in rooms with no ambience, communal tables, and the accumulated smell of eighty years of good cooking. The nihari here — slow-cooked shank of beef or mutton, cooked overnight in a sealed handi, served with nalli (marrow) and rumaali roti — is the best in Mumbai, full stop. The seekh kebabs are made on the premises with visible care. The biryani, the baida roti, and the haleem are all excellent. Sarvi's opening hours reflect its origins as a late-night and early-morning destination: it is busiest at midnight during Ramadan, when the streets outside are alive with the particular energy of a city that knows how to celebrate.
address: 109, 2nd Floor, Sarvi Building, Ibrahim Rahimtoola Marg, Bhendi Bazaar, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400003

name: Pali Bhavan
tagline: Bandra's coastal casual — honest Goan and Konkan seafood in a room that feels like a postcard from the coast.
description: Pali Bhavan in Bandra's Pali Naka neighbourhood is a neighbourhood restaurant that Bandra residents treat as a reliable constant — the kind of place where you go when you want good seafood without the formality of a reservation, the theatre of a chef's tasting menu, or the queue outside Bastian. The coastal-inspired interiors, warm service, and focus on Goan and Konkan seafood make it one of the most comfortable dining rooms in the suburb. The prawn rechado, the fish thali, and the bebinca are all excellent. The house cocktails, made with coastal ingredients like kokum and raw mango, are among the better casual cocktails in Bandra.
address: Off Pali Naka, Bandra West, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400050

name: Chaitanya
tagline: The Dadar seafood institution — where the kombdi vade is worth the traffic and the kombdi vade alone.
description: Chaitanya in Dadar is the local's answer to the tourist-friendly seafood restaurants of South Mumbai. It serves authentic Malvani food — the coastal cooking of the Konkan belt — to an audience of regulars who have been coming here for decades and will not tolerate a drop in quality. The kombdi vade (spiced chicken curry with puffed rice bread) is the most celebrated dish on the menu and one of the definitive examples of Malvani home cooking available in a restaurant setting in Mumbai. The fish curry, the prawn sukka, the sol kadhi, and the bangda fry (mackerel) are all excellent. The room is basic, the service is fast, and the prices make everything else in the city look overpriced.
address: Shivaji Mandir Marg, Dadar West, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400028

name: Wasabi by Morimoto
tagline: Iron Chef Morimoto's Japanese restaurant inside the Taj Mahal Palace — where the omakase has no rival in India.
description: Wasabi by Morimoto at the Taj Mahal Palace is the finest Japanese restaurant in India and one of the most serious in Asia. Chef Masaharu Morimoto — of Iron Chef fame — designed a restaurant that honours Japanese culinary philosophy in a city that can seem an improbable setting for it. The omakase (chef's choice) tasting menu is the way to eat here: a procession of sushi, sashimi, and cooked courses that moves through the seasons with precision. The bluefin tuna, the wagyu tartare, and the uni preparations are exceptional. The sake list is one of the most extensive in India. For the kind of dining experience that commands silence — not because it demands reverence but because the food earns it — Wasabi is in a category of its own.
address: Taj Mahal Palace Hotel, Apollo Bunder, Colaba, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400001

name: Burma Burma
tagline: The only Burmese restaurant in Mumbai — and the khow suey has been changing minds about vegetarian food since 2014.
description: Burma Burma in Fort opened in 2014 and introduced an entire city to a cuisine it had never encountered in a restaurant setting: Burmese cooking, in all its complexity. The restaurant is entirely vegetarian, which makes it an unusual fine-casual proposition — the kind of place that non-vegetarians visit specifically because the food is so compelling it makes the distinction irrelevant. The khow suey (wheat noodles in a coconut milk curry broth, served with an elaborate array of toppings that you assemble yourself at the table) is the signature, and it is extraordinary. The laphet thoke (fermented tea leaf salad) is one of the most unusual and addictive dishes available anywhere in Mumbai. The mango cooler and the warm Burmese tea are the drinks to order.
address: 20, Rustom Building, Vithal Bhai Patel Road, Fort, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400023

name: Konkan Café
tagline: The five-star Konkan kitchen — where Maharashtra's coastal heritage gets the presentation it deserves.
description: Konkan Café at the Taj Cuffe Parade (formerly the President) is the most refined expression of Konkan and coastal Maharashtrian cooking available in a luxury hotel setting in Mumbai. The restaurant has been serving this cuisine since 1994 and has helped define what 'Konkan fine dining' means for a generation of chefs. The prawn gassi (a rich coconut and tamarind curry from the Mangalorean tradition), the crab masala, the surmai recheado, and the sol kadhi are all prepared from sourced coastal ingredients and traditional recipes. The seating is elegant without being stiff, and the weekend seafood brunch is one of the finest value propositions in Mumbai hotel dining.
address: Taj Cuffe Parade, Cuffe Parade, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400005

name: Neel
tagline: The most spectacular restaurant setting in Mumbai — Indian food, Bollywood energy, and the Mahalaxmi Racecourse outside the window.
description: Neel is part of the Tote on the Turf complex at the Mahalaxmi Racecourse, and the setting alone makes it unlike any other restaurant in Mumbai. Floor-to-ceiling windows look out over the oldest horse racing track in Asia, the room is vast and dramatic, and on race days the energy is electric. But Neel earns its reputation on the food: a thoughtful modern Indian menu that draws from across the subcontinent without the gimmickry of some contemporary Indian restaurants. The butter chicken here is exceptional — not a hotel shortcut but a deeply reduced, properly smoky, precisely spiced preparation. The biryani, the kebab platter, and the regional specials that rotate by season are all excellent. The cocktail programme is one of the more ambitious in this part of the city.
address: Tote on the Turf, Mahalaxmi Racecourse, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400034

name: Gaylord
tagline: Mumbai's first fine dining restaurant — open since 1956 and still the most civilised room in Churchgate.
description: Gaylord opened in Churchgate in 1956 and was, for a generation, the most sophisticated restaurant in Bombay — a place where only the elite could afford to eat, where the menu was as continental as it was Indian, and where the standards of service set a benchmark for every restaurant that followed. Nearly seventy years later, Gaylord remains remarkable: a gracious, unhurried room with dark wood, leather banquettes, and a menu that still honours both its continental and North Indian traditions. The chicken tikka masala, the dal makhani, and the lamb rogan josh are as good as they have ever been. The pastry counter at the entrance, stocked with fresh-baked breads, éclairs, and savoury bites, is a Churchgate institution in its own right.
address: Mayfair Building, Veer Nariman Road, Churchgate, Mumbai, Maharashtra 400020

name: Indian Accent
tagline: India's most globally celebrated restaurant — where Lodhi Road's quiet grandeur meets inventive Indian brilliance.
description: Tucked inside the immaculate grounds of The Lodhi hotel on Lodhi Road, Indian Accent is the uncontested summit of fine dining in India. Founded in 2009 by Rohit Khattar and guided since inception by chef Shantanu Mehrotra — himself a protégé of the legendary Manish Mehrotra — the restaurant has held its position on Asia's 50 Best Restaurants list continuously since 2015, currently sitting at #26. What makes Indian Accent extraordinary is its complete rejection of pastiche: there are no fake antiques, no Bollywood overtures, no gimmick. Instead, the kitchen takes the flavours and techniques of the Indian subcontinent — the puchka from Calcutta's street corners, the meetha achar of Rajasthan, the morels of Kashmir — and applies to them the rigour and restraint of global fine dining. The result is a tasting menu of extraordinary precision: Puchkas with Calcutta jhal potato and five waters; meetha achar spare ribs with sour green apple; a celebrated blue cheese naan that has become the most talked-about bread in the country. The dining room is understated to the point of serenity — marble floors, white tablecloths, warm lighting — and the sommelier's wine-pairing programme is among the most thoughtful in India. Time Magazine has listed it among the 'World's 100 Greatest Places'. Booking weeks in advance is not a precaution — it is a necessity.
address: The Lodhi, Lodhi Road, New Delhi, Delhi 110003

name: Bukhara
tagline: Delhi's most legendary dining room — 45 years of tandoor smoke, head-of-state guests, and the world's most famous black lentil.
description: Bukhara, which opened at the ITC Maurya in 1977, is one of the most famous restaurants in Asia and arguably the single most influential Indian restaurant of the 20th century. It was here, in a rugged room of exposed stone walls, wooden stools, and open clay tandoors, that North-West Frontier cuisine — the cooking of what is now Afghanistan and Pakistan's Khyber Pakhtunkhwa — was first presented to Delhi's elite, and through them to the world. The menu has not changed in nearly 50 years: the culinary logic being that perfection does not require revision. Dal Bukhara — black lentils slow-cooked for 18 hours over charcoal until they collapse into a silken, smoky cream — is the most replicated dish in Indian restaurant history. Sikandari Raan, a whole leg of spring lamb marinated and roasted in the tandoor, is the grand gesture. Murgh Malai Kebab is what happens when cream, cheese, and a tandoor at exactly the right temperature meet. The dining ritual is as distinctive as the food: guests wear aprons and are expected to eat with their hands, a custom that strips away pretension and returns you to something primal. Bill Clinton came. Putin came. Sachin Tendulkar comes regularly. The legendary MF Husain painted his trademark horse on a canvas at the table during one visit. The 'Presidential Platter' was named in Clinton's honour; a 'Chelsea Platter' for his daughter. This is not a restaurant that needs to try.
address: ITC Maurya, Sardar Patel Marg, Diplomatic Enclave, Chanakyapuri, New Delhi, Delhi 110021

name: Dum Pukht
tagline: A 200-year royal legacy sealed in a handi — the grandest expression of Awadhi cuisine anywhere in India.
description: If Bukhara is the North-West Frontier in a tandoor, Dum Pukht is the court of Lucknow's Nawabs in a sealed pot. Located in the same ITC Maurya building as Bukhara but occupying an entirely different emotional register — all velvet upholstery, vintage chandeliers, hand-painted Mughal-era motifs, and the soft strains of a live sarangi — Dum Pukht was established in 1989 and has since twice appeared on the Asia's 50 Best Restaurants list. The cooking technique is the 'dum pukht' method itself: slow-roasting ingredients in dough-sealed handis over charcoal, trapping all moisture, fat, and aromatics inside the vessel until the pot is broken open at the table with a whisper of scented steam. The biryani — long-grain basmati layered with marinated lamb, saffron, and rose water, cooked sealed until every grain stands separate and perfumed — is widely considered the finest in Delhi. The kakori kebab, named for a town near Lucknow, dissolves on the tongue like something that was never solid. Koh-E-Awadh, a lamb shank in a saffron and almond gravy, is an exercise in restraint and depth. Guided since its founding by Chef Ghulam Qureshi — who learned dum cooking from his father — and built on recipes with lineages stretching back to the personal cooks of the Nawabs, Dum Pukht is not just a restaurant. It is an archive.
address: ITC Maurya, Sardar Patel Marg, Diplomatic Enclave, Chanakyapuri, New Delhi, Delhi 110021

name: Varq
tagline: Modern Indian molecular gastronomy in a room draped with Anjolie Ela Menon murals — the Taj's crown jewel.
description: Varq sits inside the legendary Taj Mahal Hotel on Mansingh Road — itself one of Delhi's most storied buildings, a diplomatic and cultural anchor for the city since 1978. The restaurant's name means 'gold leaf' in Urdu, and everything about the room reflects that precision: white tablecloths, scarlet water tumblers, the walls covered in one of India's finest privately displayed artworks — a mural cycle by painter Anjolie Ela Menon. The menu moves across the entirety of India without losing its footing: Bengali crab preparations sit alongside Chettinad-spiced chicken, Haleem with crisp kebabs, and a Dal Varq that is the restaurant's signature lentil composition. What distinguishes Varq from more conventional hotel fine dining is its molecular sensibility — the kitchen uses modern technique not for spectacle but to bring Indian ingredients into sharper focus. The gandheri chicken kebab, the atta raan (a roast leg of lamb encased in whole-wheat dough), and the jalebis with silver leaves and pistachio dip have all been celebrated in the Asia's 50 Best extended list. The sommelier's programme is one of the strongest in any Delhi hotel. For a dinner that spans regions and reminds you of the breadth of Indian cuisine, Varq is the most comprehensive answer in the capital.
address: The Taj Mahal Hotel, No. 1, Mansingh Road, New Delhi, Delhi 110011
mustTry: Jalebi with Silver Leaves & Pistachio Dip

name: Olive Bar & Kitchen
tagline: A restored Mughal-era haveli, an old banyan tree, and the best Mediterranean table in South Delhi.
description: There is no more enchanting setting for dinner in Delhi than Olive Bar & Kitchen in Mehrauli. The restaurant occupies a beautifully restored 16th-century haveli at the foot of the Qutub Minar — a place where whitewashed walls, arched windows, and an ancient banyan tree draped in fairy lights create a courtyard that feels genuinely transported out of the capital's noise. Founded by restaurateur A.D. Singh, Olive opened in 2002 and has been a consistent benchmark for European fine dining in Delhi ever since. The menu is broadly Mediterranean — stone-fired pizzas with wood-sourced mushrooms and truffle oil, handmade pastas, butter-poached prawn risotto, burrata tarts with slow-roasted tomatoes — and the kitchen executes it with a precision that is rarely found this far from the source. On weekend evenings, when the courtyard fills with candlelight, live music drifts through the garden, and the faint outline of the Qutub Minar is visible from the terrace, there is a strong argument that Olive offers the single most romantic setting in the city. The cocktail programme, anchored by the Mehrauli Mule and a thoughtful gin list, is among the more creative in South Delhi. Reservations for the courtyard tables are essential on weekends and during Delhi's cooler months, when the garden comes into its own.
address: One Style Mile, Haveli No. 6-8, Kalka Das Marg, Mehrauli, New Delhi, Delhi 110030

name: Karim's
tagline: Mughal royal recipes served to the common man since 1913 — Old Delhi's most sacred culinary institution.
description: In 1911, when Delhi Durbar was held for the coronation of King George V, a former cook from the Mughal imperial court named Haji Karimuddin looked at the thousands flooding into Delhi from across the subcontinent and had a vision: to bring the royal kitchen to the street. Two years later, in a narrow lane behind Jama Masjid called Gali Kababian, he established the original Karim Hotel, declaring his intent simply: 'I want to earn fame and money by serving the royal food to the common man.' He succeeded beyond any reasonable expectation. More than a century later, the fourth generation of the Karim family — under Aivaz Asif — still runs the original restaurant in exactly the place and largely in the spirit that Haji Karimuddin intended. The room is no-frills by design: zinc-topped tables, crowded communal seating, the low hum of a kitchen that never quite stops. The food, however, is magnificent. Mutton Nihari — the slow-cooked shin stew that was once served to Mughal courtiers before dawn — is the signature dish, a broth of extraordinary depth and fragrance. The Seekh Kebabs are textbook: minced lamb bound with spices and onion, grilled over coal, smoky and yielding. Mutton Burrah (marinated bone-in chops cooked in the tandoor) loses its meat at the first bite. The Chicken Jahangiri, named for the Mughal emperor, is a rich, fragrant composite. Getting there is half the experience: the restaurant is tucked into a lane so narrow that Jama Masjid's minarets appear to float directly overhead as you walk in.
address: 16, Gali Kababian, Near Jama Masjid Gate No. 1, Jama Masjid, Old Delhi, New Delhi, Delhi 110006

name: Moti Mahal
tagline: Birthplace of butter chicken and tandoori chicken — the original restaurant that changed how the world eats Indian food.
description: There is almost no dish more globally familiar than butter chicken, and its story begins here, at Moti Mahal in Daryaganj, in a year somewhere between 1947 and 1950. The restaurant was re-established in Delhi by Kundan Lal Gujral (and his partners) after Partition, in the neighbourhood where old Delhi meets new — Daryaganj, a busy, sprawling intersection of history and commerce. The origin story of murgh makhani is straightforward and human: the kitchen had leftover tandoori chicken at the end of service, and someone — Gujral's descendants say it was Gujral himself — suggested adding butter and tomato to the marinade tray drippings, tossing the chicken through the result, and serving it. The dish that emerged was so balanced — creamy, tangy, fragrant with aromatic char — that it became permanent on the menu the next day, and from Daryaganj it spread to every Indian restaurant on every continent. Jawaharlal Nehru was a devoted patron; Indira Gandhi, Richard Nixon, and Jackie Kennedy all came. The food today — butter chicken, dal makhani (also invented here), tandoori chicken — remains exactly what it was: the definitive North Indian restaurant canon, prepared with the confidence of a kitchen that has never needed to prove itself. A meal at Moti Mahal is not a discovery. It is a homecoming.
address: 3703, Netaji Subhash Marg, Daryaganj, New Delhi, Delhi 110002

name: Gulati
tagline: Pandara Road's beloved old hand — a 1959 dhaba grown into Delhi's most reliable North Indian dining room.
description: Pandara Road Market, tucked behind India Gate in Central Delhi, is one of the city's great dining destinations — a strip of restaurants where middle-class Delhi has been eating well since the 1950s. Among the cluster of names that occupy this stretch, Gulati is the most beloved and the most consistent. It began as a dhaba with wooden benches and tables in 1959, and over 65 years has evolved into a spacious, ornate restaurant with richly decorated interiors, a menu that spans 175 recipes, and a lunch buffet that draws families from across the city. The Butter Chicken here has been voted best-in-Delhi by multiple polls — rich, smooth, properly spiced, neither over-sweetened nor abridged. The Dal Makhani is deep and slow. The Kakori Kebab, a Lucknowi speciality of minced lamb and rose water formed around a skewer, is one of the lightest on the palate in the city. The Hyderabadi dum pukht biryanis are aromatic and properly layered. For vegetarians, the Paneer Lajawab and the Khazana platter represent the kitchen at its best. The restaurant does not serve alcohol but compensates with an excellent range of mocktails and fresh-pressed juices. On weekend evenings, queues outside Gulati are entirely normal — a testament to how stubbornly well it has maintained its standards.
address: 6, Pandara Road Market, Near India Gate, New Delhi, Delhi 110003

name: The Grammar Room
tagline: The thinking person's neighbourhood restaurant — a place to return to over and over again.
description: The Grammar Room in Vasant Vihar has built an unusual reputation in Delhi: a restaurant that is not trying to impress anyone, and which is consistently excellent for precisely that reason. The space is handsome — warm tones, good lighting, a relaxed energy that is neither too casual nor overdressed — and it has stood the test of time in a city where restaurants open and close with alarming speed. The menu is modern Indian with an intelligent pan-Asian undercurrent: dishes that take familiar comfort-food traditions and refine them without stripping away their warmth. Time Out Delhi has returned to it repeatedly as one of the most dependable restaurants in the city. The cocktail programme is thoughtful and well-executed. It is the kind of restaurant that builds loyal regulars rather than chasing Instagram visitors, and its neighbourhood following — Vasant Vihar's professional class, young families, and returning Delhiites — says everything about the trust it has earned.
address: 9, Local Shopping Centre, Vasant Vihar, New Delhi, Delhi 110057

name: Dakshin
tagline: The definitive South Indian fine dining table in Delhi — five states, one room, and a menu that dismantles every stereotype.
description: Dakshin at the ITC Maurya occupies a position in Delhi's South Indian dining that Indian Accent occupies in the city's modern Indian landscape: it is the benchmark, the reference point, the place other restaurants are compared against. The restaurant takes its name from the Sanskrit for 'south' and its mission seriously — the menu spans Kerala, Karnataka, Andhra Pradesh, Tamil Nadu, and Chettinad with a rigour that lesser establishments rarely attempt. It dismantles the persistent misconception that South Indian cuisine is inherently vegetarian: veinchina mamsam (lamb with coriander and garam masala), deep-fried Malabar prawns, Meen Moilee (a delicate Kerala fish curry in coconut milk), and Chicken Chettinad (fierce, peppery, irreducibly aromatic) all argue eloquently for the opposite. The room is appropriately theatrical — murals, temple-inspired motifs, traditional bronzework — and the service follows the calibration expected of an ITC fine dining property. The lunch thali is one of the most comprehensive South Indian spreads available in a five-star setting. For those who approach South Indian cuisine only through idli-sambar and filter coffee, Dakshin is a corrective education.
address: ITC Maurya, Sardar Patel Marg, Diplomatic Enclave, Chanakyapuri, New Delhi, Delhi 110021

name: Shang Palace
tagline: Delhi's finest Cantonese room — opulent lacquerwork, impeccable dim sum, and the best Peking duck in the capital.
description: Shang Palace at the Shangri-La Eros in Connaught Place is Delhi's definitive answer to the question of what a world-class Chinese fine dining restaurant looks like in India. The room is deliberately designed in the grand Cantonese tradition — rich lacquerwork, carved screens, traditional lanterns, private dining pods separated by latticed dividers — and it carries that aesthetic through to the table with flawless service, fine chinaware, and a wine list assembled with attention to Chinese food pairing. The dim sum programme — har gow, siu mai, cheung fun, and a rotating selection of steamed and baked dumplings — is widely acknowledged as the strongest in Delhi, each piece made to order with skins of the correct weight and fillings precisely seasoned. The Peking duck is carved tableside with ceremony, the first course of pancakes and hoisin followed by the second service of stir-fried duck meat with lettuce cups. The live seafood tank ensures freshness of the Cantonese whole-fish preparations, and the wok-fried lobster in black bean sauce is the room's showpiece. LBB ranked it at the top of their 2025 Delhi restaurant mega-list.
address: Shangri-La Eros New Delhi, 19, Ashoka Road, Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001
mustTry: Wok-Fried Lobster in Black Bean Sauce

name: Paranthe Wali Gali
tagline: The lane that has been frying stuffed parathas since 1875 — Old Delhi's most famous address for breakfast.
description: Paranthe Wali Gali — literally 'the lane of parathas' — is a narrow alley in Chandni Chowk that has been cooking stuffed parathas on cast-iron tavas since the 1870s, and some of the stalls claim family lineages going back five or six generations. It is not a restaurant in any conventional sense: there are no tables as such, just shallow ledges, plastic stools spilling into the alley, and a continuous soundtrack of sizzling ghee. What the lane offers is something far more specific than 'a paratha': each stall produces parathas stuffed with improbable fillings — khurchan (dried milk solids), rabri (sweetened condensed milk), dried fruits, spiced keema, paneer, potato and chilli, banana and jaggery — and serves them with a rotating cast of chutneys, pickles, and sweetened yoghurt. The experience is part meal, part living museum. Tourists come, yes, but so do Old Delhi residents who have been eating here since childhood. The best stalls — Pandit Kanhaiyalal & Sons and its neighbours — are easy to identify by the queue. Come before noon, and come hungry: it is harder to leave without eating three than it was to eat the first one.
address: Paranthe Wali Gali (Gali Paranthe Wali), Chandni Chowk, Old Delhi, New Delhi, Delhi 110006

name: Masala Library by Jiggs Kalra
tagline: India's most technically daring Indian restaurant — molecular gastronomy applied to the subcontinent's deepest flavours.
description: Masala Library by Jiggs Kalra, located in the First International Finance Centre in BKC (and with its original flagship in Mumbai), represents a distinct and unapologetic position in the Indian fine dining landscape: the belief that Indian cuisine can and should be subjected to the same rigour of molecular gastronomy that has transformed European fine dining. Chef Jiggs Kalra and his team deploy liquid nitrogen, spherification, edible films, and precision temperature control not as theatre but as tools for intensifying and clarifying Indian flavour profiles. The signature dish — butter chicken served with naan foam — reduces India's most beloved curry to its elemental textures while preserving every note of the marinade and sauce. The slow-cooked lamb dishes, the contemporary chaat preparations with inverted textures, and the dessert section — which typically features a deconstructed Indian mithai — are all executed with a precision that demands respect. The room is appropriately dramatic: dark tones, theatrical lighting, and a room that feels designed to frame a performance. For those who find Indian Accent too restrained or Bukhara too traditional, Masala Library offers the most explicit reimagination of the canon.
address: Ground Floor, Vatika Business Centre, Statesman House, 148, Barakhamba Road, Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

name: The Spice Route
tagline: One of the most beautiful restaurants in the world — a mural-covered odyssey through Asia's spice route inside The Imperial.
description: The Spice Route, located inside The Imperial Hotel on Janpath, is widely considered one of the most visually extraordinary restaurant interiors on the planet. Designed by cultural curator Rajeev Sethi over seven years, the room was painted entirely by mural artists from a single hamlet in Kerala using natural vegetable and flower dyes — every wall, ceiling, and column depicting the eastward journey of spices from the Malabar Coast through Sri Lanka, Burma, Thailand, and Vietnam. The architecture borrows from Hindu temple structure: 16th-century stone pillars divide the space into sections that metaphorically narrate the journey of life, from birth through to the ancestors waiting at the end. The cuisine follows the same geographical arc — Kerala-style prawns in coconut, Tom Yum with tiger prawns, Sri Lankan crab curry, Vietnamese spring rolls, stir-fried lobster with ginger and mushrooms — each kitchen section handled by chefs trained in that specific regional tradition. The Imperial itself, a colonial-era art deco masterpiece built in 1931, provides a setting that no purpose-built restaurant could replicate. The Spice Route is not just dinner; it is an architectural event. Reservations are essential, and first-time visitors are strongly encouraged to request the full restaurant tour before sitting down.
address: The Imperial Hotel, Janpath Lane, Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

name: Loya
tagline: A grand North Indian feast from the Himalayas to Punjab — Taj Palace's most celebrated dining room.
description: Loya at the Taj Palace Hotel in Chanakyapuri takes its name from the Pashtun word for a grand council of tribal leaders who convene over a feast — and the restaurant lives up to that ambition entirely. The menu traces a culinary arc across India's rugged north: from the morel-scented valleys of Kashmir and the walnut-smoked preparations of Himachal to the mustard fields of Punjab and the flame-grilled frontier traditions of the North-West. The glass-fronted live kitchen is a theatre in itself — guests watch kebabs being drawn from the tandoor, spices being pounded, and pots simmering with aromatic curries. The cocktail programme is among the most inventive in any Delhi hotel, organised around five philosophical moods — Harmony, Experience, Authentic, Revered, and The Spirit — and executed with a precision that matches the kitchen. LBB named it a highlight of their 2025 Delhi mega-list for its 'unforgettable, packed with flavours' cooking and theatrical tableside service. The Dal Ki Chaat, finished at your table; the Raan-e-Jigra, torched in front of you; the Timbri Jhinga marinated with shrub seed and pahadi bhaang jeera chutney — each dish has a layer of ceremony that elevates the occasion. This is one of the finest luxury North Indian experiences in the capital.
address: Taj Palace, Diplomatic Enclave, Sardar Patel Marg, Chanakyapuri, New Delhi, Delhi 110021

name: Chor Bizarre
tagline: A cabinet of curiosities in a 1956 landmark — Delhi's most eccentric room and its finest Kashmiri table.
description: Chor Bizarre — literally 'Thieves' Market' — opened in 1990 inside Hotel Broadway on Asaf Ali Road, the historic street that joins Old Delhi to New Delhi. Founded by Rohit Khattar (the same family behind Indian Accent), the restaurant was built around his personal collection of salvaged household curiosities: vintage typewriters, old gramophones, antique clocks, a full 1920s Fiat car parked inside the room and repurposed as the salad bar. The result is one of Delhi's most memorable dining rooms — chaotic, warm, and genuinely unlike anywhere else. The food is the serious counterpart to the eccentric decor: a proper archive of Kashmiri Wazwan, the royal banquet tradition of the Kashmir Valley. Rogan Josh — lamb cooked in fennel, ginger, and Kashmiri chilli — is the standard-bearer. Mutton Nihari is slow-cooked overnight to a silken depth. Nadru Yakhni (lotus stem in yoghurt and fennel gravy) is the vegetarian showpiece. Haak Saag, the simple stir of Kashmiri greens with asafoetida, is what the regulars eat. For tourists wanting to experience the character of Old Delhi without sacrificing comfort or quality, Chor Bizarre occupies a unique position in the city.
address: Hotel Broadway, 4/15A, Asaf Ali Road, New Delhi, Delhi 110002

name: Parikrama — The Revolving Restaurant
tagline: India's highest revolving restaurant — 240 feet above Connaught Place, with all of Delhi turning slowly beneath you.
description: Parikrama is Delhi's only revolving restaurant and India's highest — a dining room mounted on the 24th floor of a commercial tower on Kasturba Gandhi Marg, 240 feet above the city centre. The concept is simple and completely effective: the room completes one full revolution every 90 minutes, and in the course of a single meal you will see the Red Fort, Jama Masjid, Rashtrapati Bhavan, India Gate, Humayun's Tomb, and the geometric sweep of Connaught Place pass by the floor-to-ceiling windows. It is, unambiguously, the most dramatic panoramic dining experience in the capital. The menu spans North Indian, Mughlai, Chinese, and Continental preparations — biryanis, kebabs, curries, pasta — executed to a consistent and reliable standard. Parikrama is not trying to be Indian Accent or Bukhara: its distinction is the experience, and on that score it is entirely without competition in Delhi. For first-time visitors to the capital, a dinner at Parikrama — ideally at sunset, when the city shifts from gold to amber to the constellation of evening lights — is one of the defining experiences the city offers.
address: 22, Kasturba Gandhi Marg, Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

name: Rajinder Da Dhaba
tagline: From a single fish-fry stall in 1968 to South Delhi's most loved culinary institution — a rags-to-riches dhaba legend.
description: Rajinder Da Dhaba occupies an entire market block in Safdarjung Enclave, and the story of how it got there is one of Delhi's great food narratives. It began in 1968 as a modest rekhdi (street cart) on Kamal Cinema Road, started by the late Shri Rajinder Kumar Dua, selling nothing more ambitious than fish fry and egg fry. The hand-ground spices and uncompromising quality drew regulars, then pilgrims from across the city. By the 2000s it had expanded beyond any ordinary dhaba: today, RDD — as it is universally known — comprises a fine-dining section, a casual sit-down hall, a takeaway counter of staggering throughput, and the legendary parking lot experience where Delhiites stand beside their motorcycles and cars, eating off the bonnet at 10pm on a Tuesday. The Afghani Chicken is the signature and the reason food writers travel here: marinated in thick yoghurt and cream, seasoned with hand-ground spices, roasted in the tandoor until the skin chars and the meat practically falls — one of the finest preparations of chicken in the city. The Dal Makhani is deep and generous. Politicians, Bollywood stars, and generations of South Delhi families have all been coming since before it was famous. The third generation of the Dua family now runs it, and the recipes haven't moved.
address: AB-14B, Nauroji Nagar Marg, Safdarjung Enclave Market, New Delhi, Delhi 110029

name: United Coffee House
tagline: Connaught Place's grand old dining room since 1942 — chandeliers, stained glass, and the best people-watching in the city.
description: United Coffee House on E-Block, Connaught Place, is one of Delhi's oldest continuously operating restaurants, and in certain respects one of its most irreplaceable. The dining room dates to 1942 and has barely changed: crystal chandeliers hang from high ceilings, stained-glass panels divide the room, dark wood panelling absorbs the decades, and the mix of humanity that comes here — students, bureaucrats, foreign visitors, politicians, artists, couples on first dates and couples on fortieth anniversaries — is a living cross-section of the capital. The menu is deliberately multi-cuisine and deliberately ambitious: biryani sits alongside club sandwiches and pasta, chicken tikka beside prawn cocktail and a Thai green curry. It is not trying to be the best at any single thing; it is trying to be reliably good at everything, and largely succeeds. For a long afternoon of coffee, conversation, and people-watching with a gin and tonic and a bowl of lamb shorba, there is no more comfortable room in the city. It has been a Connaught Place institution for over 80 years, and it shows no sign of changing — which is precisely the point.
address: E-15, Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

name: Saravana Bhavan
tagline: The world's most famous South Indian chain — and the Janpath outpost is Delhi's definitive address for dosa and filter coffee.
description: Saravana Bhavan is one of the most remarkable restaurant stories of the 20th century: a single idli-dosa counter opened in Chennai in 1981 that became a global institution with outlets on five continents. The Janpath outlet in New Delhi is the capital's most beloved South Indian address for a large section of the city's population — particularly the enormous Tamil and Malayali communities of the capital — and a revelation for those who associate South Indian food only with the greasy corridor restaurants of North Indian neighbourhoods. The menu is the full Tamil canon: masala dosa with precisely spiced potato filling and a paper-thin, properly fermented crêpe; idli that arrives warm and cloud-soft with sambar and coconut chutney; Pongal, the rice-and-lentil comfort dish, available every morning; Ven Pongal with ghee and pepper; onion uthappam; and the legendary Saravana Special Meals — a south Indian thali that changes daily and arrives in waves of small steel cups. The filter coffee is served in the traditional style: poured between tumbler and davara, strong and chicory-sweet. At under ₹300 a head for a full meal, Saravana Bhavan offers arguably the best value-per-rupee dining experience available in the capital.
address: 46, Janpath, Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

name: Haldiram's
tagline: India's most iconic snack institution — the Chandni Chowk original where Delhi's best chaat and mithai meet under one roof.
