description: Haldiram's needs no introduction to anyone who grew up in India: founded in Bikaner in 1937, it is the country's most recognised name in packaged snacks, sweets, and fast-casual Indian food. But the Chandni Chowk flagship in Old Delhi is something categorically different from the packaged-goods brand: it is a full-service, bustling dining hall where generations of Delhi families have come for chaat, mithai, South Indian breakfast, and thali. The sweets counter alone — rows upon rows of barfi, gulab jamun, jalebi, rasgulla, ladoo, and the Bikaneri specialities the brand was built on — is a pilgrimage destination. The Raj Kachori, a fried wheat shell filled with potato, sprouts, chutneys, yoghurt, and sev, is possibly the most complete single-bite experience available in the city. The papdi chaat is textbook. The South Indian section offers reliable masala dosa and filter coffee at prices that have barely moved. Rajma chawal, chole bhature, and seasonal specials round out a menu that serves every demographic and every budget. For tourists wanting a single address in Chandni Chowk that captures the breadth of Indian snacking culture without the navigation of the galis, Haldiram's is the answer.
address: 1454/2, Chandni Chowk, Old Delhi, New Delhi, Delhi 110006

name: Naivedyam
tagline: Hauz Khas Village's beloved South Indian kitchen — temple-style purity, perfect dosas, and the best filter coffee in South Delhi.
description: Naivedyam in Hauz Khas Village occupies a curious and beloved position in Delhi's dining landscape: a strictly vegetarian, no-alcohol South Indian restaurant in one of the city's most fashionable creative neighbourhoods, surrounded by bars and concept restaurants, drawing long queues every weekend on the strength of its food alone. The name means 'offering to God' in Sanskrit — a reference to the temple-kitchen tradition of South India where food is prepared without onion or garlic and served as prasad. The kitchen honours that tradition: the sambhar is made without onion, relying on tamarind, lentil, and dried chilli for its depth, and the result is cleaner and more aromatic than most. The masala dosa has a crêpe of impeccable fermentation, the filling properly spiced and restrained. The Ghee Roast Dosa — a dosa brushed with clarified butter until it becomes almost lacy and crisp — is the signature preparation. The full South Indian meals (available at lunch) arrive on banana leaf with rice, three vegetables, rasam, sambhar, papad, and payasam, and represent some of the best-value eating in the city. Naivedyam has been a consistent favourite on every serious Delhi food list for over two decades.
address: 1, Hauz Khas Village, New Delhi, Delhi 110016

name: Sagar Ratna
tagline: Delhi's original South Indian institution — the Defence Colony founding outlet that started a national chain in 1986.
description: Sagar Ratna is to South Indian vegetarian dining in Delhi what Karim's is to Mughlai — the original, the touchstone, the restaurant every subsequent establishment is measured against. Founded in 1986 in Defence Colony by the late P.L. Garg, it began as a small and entirely unpretentious South Indian restaurant serving dosas, idlis, and filter coffee to a neighbourhood that had no such thing. Within years it had drawn pilgrims from across the city, and within a decade it had expanded into a chain that today operates across India. But the Defence Colony original retains its primacy: the sambhar recipe, the coconut chutney ratio, the fermentation culture for the dosas — all are maintained to the standard the founder set. The Ghee Podi Dosa — a dosa smeared with a dry powder of roasted lentils, chilli, and sesame, then finished with clarified butter — is the Defence Colony signature. The Uttapam, thick and soft with onion and tomato pressed into the batter, is textbook. The filter coffee is served in the traditional manner. For the neighbourhoods of South Delhi, Sagar Ratna is not a restaurant choice — it is a habit.
address: 18, Defence Colony Market, Defence Colony, New Delhi, Delhi 110024

name: Inja
tagline: Delhi's most exciting new fine-dining address — an Indian-Japanese fusion that shouldn't work but absolutely does.
description: Inja is the most talked-about new fine-dining arrival in Delhi in recent years — an experimental restaurant by Chef Adwait Anantwar that fuses Indian and Japanese culinary traditions in a way that, on paper, sounds improbable and, in the eating, is completely convincing. The kitchen finds the structural parallels between the two cuisines — the shared reverence for fermentation, for precision knife work, for the transformation of modest ingredients into something transcendent — and builds a tasting menu around the intersections. The Udon Khasi Curry is the signature: Japanese udon noodles in a Northeast Indian mutton broth that has enough in common with a Japanese dashi-based ramen that the combination feels inevitable rather than forced. Shiso Leaf Banarasi Chaat brings the acidic, herbal complexity of Japanese shiso into the chaat grammar of sweet, sour, spice, and crunch. The setting is intimate and deliberately designed — dark tones, counter seating facing the open kitchen, a focused wine and sake list. LBB described it as a restaurant that 'lives rent-free in your brain'. Booking in advance is essential; tables are limited and demand has been consistent since opening.
address: S-477, Greater Kailash II, New Delhi, Delhi 110048

name: Kake Da Hotel
tagline: The dal-splashed windows of Connaught Place's most legendary dhaba — where Delhi's taxi drivers and cabinet ministers eat at the same table.
description: Kake Da Hotel is one of the most quietly famous restaurants in India. Located on Connaught Place's inner circle, recognisable by the characteristic dal stains that have worked their way into the exterior paintwork over decades of service, it has been feeding Delhi since 1931. The formula is unchanged: a short menu of Punjabi standards — Chicken Bhurji Masala (known to regulars as 'Chicken Bhayankar'), Dal Makhani, Mutton Curry, a simple salad of onion and lemon — served in a room with no ambitions beyond feeding you well. The vegetable biryani has its devoted constituency. The naan comes out of the tandoor with the correct char. Kake Da Hotel is celebrated not because it tries to be anything it isn't, but because it has spent nearly a century being exactly one thing: a reliable, generous, completely unpretentious Punjabi table. Politicians, Bollywood actors, foreign diplomats, and auto-rickshaw drivers have all eaten here, and the room makes no distinctions. It is among the most democratic institutions in Connaught Place.
address: 74, Inner Circle, Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

name: The Orient Express
tagline: Board the most famous train in history for dinner — a 40-year-old Taj Palace institution where Europe arrives course by course.
description: The Orient Express at the Taj Palace Hotel is one of Delhi's most singular dining experiences: a European fine-dining restaurant designed entirely as an immersive recreation of a first-class compartment on the legendary train, from the polished wood panelling and brass fittings of the dining cars to the fringed upholstery and compartment-style seating. In operation for over 40 years, it has accumulated a devoted following among both Delhi's diplomatic community in Chanakyapuri and international visitors wanting a formal European meal in an extraordinary setting. The menu traces the route of the train — hors d'oeuvres inspired by the cooking of Paris, entrées from Vienna and Budapest, cheeses and desserts from across the continent — and the kitchen sources ingredients from European specialists with remarkable care: the caviar, lobster, crab, and camembert are all provenance-specific. The service is formal and perfectly calibrated. An evening at The Orient Express is, by design, a step outside India entirely — an exercise in culinary escapism that few restaurants in the world can replicate as convincingly.
address: Taj Palace, Diplomatic Enclave, Sardar Patel Marg, Chanakyapuri, New Delhi, Delhi 110021

name: Nathu's Sweets
tagline: Bengali Market's sweet institution since 1939 — Delhi's favourite address for rasgulla, chole bhature, and nostalgia.
description: Nathu's Sweets in Bengali Market — the charming small bazaar tucked between Connaught Place and Mandi House — has been feeding Delhi since 1939. The Bengali Market area itself is one of the city's great casual eating destinations, a tightly packed cluster of sweet shops, chaat stalls, and lunch counters that has resisted the city's relentless commercial transformation. Nathu's is the anchor: a full-service sweet shop and snack restaurant with a glass counter of extraordinary variety — Bengali rasgulla and sandesh sit alongside Punjabi barfi, Rajasthani ghevar, and freshly fried jalebis. The savoury counter is equally compelling: the Chole Bhature — puffed deep-fried bread with a spiced chickpea curry — is widely considered among the finest in the city. The aloo tikki, the papdi chaat, and the kachori sabzi are all calibrated to the Delhi palate with decades of practice. Nathu's is the address where Delhiites who grew up in Connaught Place have been bringing their children and grandchildren for generations, and the reason Bengali Market continues to draw visitors from across the city.
address: Bengali Market, Mandi House Area, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

name: Diggin
tagline: Brunch inside a Maharaja's palace — the most charming outdoor terrace in Central Delhi.
description: Diggin at Bikaner House is one of the most unusual and enchanting restaurant settings in Delhi. Bikaner House was constructed in 1929 by the British Raj as the Delhi winter palace of the Maharaja Sir Ganga Singh of Bikaner — a grand Art Deco building at the southern edge of Princes' Park, just off Pandara Road. The building was restored to its original splendour and Diggin was established as its primary restaurant, taking over several of the palace's outdoor terraces and garden spaces. On a winter morning, the experience is genuinely transportive: breakfast and brunch on a stone terrace, under trees, surrounded by Art Deco arches and a carefully restored Raj-era garden. The menu is European-leaning all-day cafe fare — eggs Benedict, wood-fired pizzas, pasta, burgers, excellent coffee — executed with care and consistency. The avocado toast and shakshuka are weekend morning mainstays. The garden terrace fills rapidly on weekend mornings; arriving before 10am is advisable in the cooler months. Diggin has multiple locations across Delhi, but the Bikaner House original — the one set inside a prince's palace — is the one to visit.
address: Bikaner House, Pandara Road, India Gate Area, New Delhi, Delhi 110003

name: Tamra
tagline: The Shangri-La's legendary all-day dining room — Delhi's most lavish international buffet spread.
description: Tamra at the Shangri-La Eros in Connaught Place is Delhi's most celebrated all-day dining restaurant — a sweeping multi-cuisine buffet hall that operates across breakfast, lunch, and dinner with a spread that draws both hotel guests and the city's most loyal buffet devotees. Named for the Sanskrit word for 'copper', the room is designed around live kitchen stations: a tandoor section with freshly made Indian breads and grills, a South-East Asian wok station, a Japanese corner with sushi and maki, an extensive chaat and Indian street food counter, a carvery, and a dessert station that alone justifies the cover charge. The kitchen's strength lies in its consistency — at a Connaught Place five-star, maintaining the quality of 40-odd dishes simultaneously across three meal services is a significant achievement, and Tamra does it reliably. The Sunday brunch, with live music and a bottomless drinks package, is one of the most popular fixed-date events in Delhi's five-star dining calendar. For business travellers, large groups, or families with wildly different palates, Tamra solves every problem at once.
address: Shangri-La Eros New Delhi, 19, Ashoka Road, Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001

name: Mamagoto
tagline: Delhi's most beloved Pan-Asian casual — possibly the best dim sum in town and a cocktail list to match.
description: Mamagoto — the name is Japanese for 'playing with food' — is one of Delhi's most consistently celebrated casual restaurants, and one of the few that has built a loyal following on pure food quality rather than spectacle or novelty. The menu is an intelligent Pan-Asian sweep: Japanese-style sushi and maki, Hong Kong-style dim sum, wok-fried Thai preparations, Vietnamese-inflected noodles, and the most talked-about dumplings in the city. The dim sum programme — steamed, pan-fried, and baked preparations rotating with the season — is regularly ranked the best in Delhi by both food critics and LBB's annual lists. The Wan Chai Chicken (slow-braised in a Cantonese master stock) and the Chilli Tofu (crisp, assertive, properly spicy) are menu permanents with dedicated followings. The cocktail bar is genuinely good — creative Asian-inflected preparations that match the food rather than compete with it. Multiple locations operate across Delhi NCR, but the original Vasant Kunj flagship retains the best atmosphere. From corporate dinners to couples' nights, Mamagoto handles every occasion without trying too hard.
address: Shop 4, Ambawatta Complex, Kalkaji, New Delhi, Delhi 110019

name: Oh! Calcutta
tagline: Delhi's definitive Bengali dining room — fish curry, mustard prawns, and the most homesick-curing kosha mangsho in the capital.
description: Oh! Calcutta at Nehru Place is Delhi's most serious address for authentic Bengali cuisine — a restaurant with a singular obsession and the consistent execution to back it up. The kitchen is built around the food of Bengal's middle-class home: the repertoire of fresh-water fish preparations, slow-braised mutton dishes, and subtly spiced vegetarian sides that define one of India's great regional culinary traditions. Hilsa — the beloved river fish of Bengal, rich with fat and bone and revered by Bengalis with something approaching religious feeling — is served here in several preparations, including the definitive Shorshe Ilish (hilsa in a mustard and green chilli paste), available when the seasonal catch permits. The Chingri Malai Curry (tiger prawns in a delicate coconut milk gravy, barely spiced, the sweetness of the prawn preserved) is the permanent signature. Kosha Mangsho — mutton cooked slowly in its own fat with caramelised onion until the sauce becomes almost dry and deeply concentrated — is the definitive Bengali comfort dish, and Oh! Calcutta's version is the reason the city's Bengali diaspora travels to Nehru Place specifically for dinner.
address: E Block, International Trade Towers, Nehru Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110019

name: Swagath
tagline: Delhi's best coastal seafood table — Mangalorean fish curry and Chettinad crab for a landlocked city that learned to love the coast.
description: Swagath in Defence Colony is one of the most quietly excellent restaurants in South Delhi — a coastal Indian seafood specialist that has spent over two decades building a devoted following among the capital's South Indian community and among anyone who has discovered that some of India's finest seafood cooking happens far from the coast. The kitchen's speciality is Mangalorean and Kerala-style preparations: fish in coconut-based gravies of great complexity, prawn dishes with curry leaf and kokum, crab masala that is fierce and fragrant without overwhelming the sweetness of the crab itself. The Mangalorean Fish Curry — red fish curry with a coconut-tamarind base, served with Neer Dosa — is the signature, a dish that tastes like it was made in a home kitchen in Udupi. The Crab Gassi (Mangalorean crab in coconut gravy) is among the most frequently cited must-eat dishes in Delhi food writing. The room is understated and the service unpretentious. Swagath rewards those who seek it out rather than those drawn by spectacle — which is precisely the kind of restaurant that develops the deepest loyalty.
address: 14, Defence Colony Market, Defence Colony, New Delhi, Delhi 110024
mustTry: Mangalorean Fish Curry with Neer Dosa

name: Wenger's Deli
tagline: Delhi's oldest bakery since 1926 — the patisserie counter that outlasted empires, Partition, and a century of Connaught Place change.
description: Wenger's Deli on A-Block, Connaught Place, is the oldest surviving bakery in Delhi, founded in 1926 by a Swiss confectioner named Wenger who arrived in India to serve the European community of the colonial capital. Nearly a century later, it remains exactly what it was: a narrow, unpretentious bakery counter with glass cases of pastries, cakes, patties, sandwiches, and the best cold coffee in Connaught Place. The Chicken Patty — a short-crust pastry case filled with spiced minced chicken and onion, still made to the original recipe — is the singular most famous baked good in Delhi. The plum cake at Christmas, the cream rolls and eclairs on ordinary afternoons, and the sausage rolls that disappear by noon are all prepared in the same tradition. Wenger's does not attempt to be a restaurant; it is a takeaway counter and has always been one. But it has endured every upheaval the city has thrown at it — Partition, Independence, the transformation of Connaught Place around it — and remains the most historically significant bakery in the country. The queue at lunch on a weekday tells you everything.
address: A-16, Connaught Place, New Delhi, Delhi 110001
